text: Breaking a task into 2-minute chunks makes it far more likely you'll actually start.
The 'two-minute rule': if it takes less than two minutes, do it now instead of scheduling it.
Willpower works like a muscle - it tires with use but also grows stronger with practice.
Writing a task down, even a tiny one, makes you roughly 40% more likely to complete it.
Streaks work because your brain treats 'don't break the chain' as loss aversion, not just gain.
Task-switching costs up to 40% of your productive time - finishing one thing before starting the next pays off.
The Zeigarnik effect: unfinished tasks stick in your memory and cause background stress until closed out.
A five-minute warm-up on a hard task is often enough to dissolve the resistance to starting it.
Habits form faster when they're attached to an existing routine, not a new time slot.
Physical movement for 60 seconds can measurably reset focus after a mental block.
Progress, even 1%, compounds - it's the direction that matters more than the size of the step.
Decision fatigue is real: tackling your hardest task earlier in the day usually goes better.
Public or even self-declared commitments increase follow-through by making the goal feel real.
Clutter in your workspace measurably increases the time it takes to start a task.
Short breaks between tasks help consolidate what you just did into memory.
Naming exactly what 'done' looks like before starting cuts task time significantly.
Social tasks feel lighter when scheduled instead of left open-ended - vague plans get skipped.
Skill practice sticks best in short, frequent sessions rather than rare long ones.
A tidy first five minutes on a chore often carries momentum through the rest of it.
Celebrating small wins releases dopamine that reinforces the habit loop for next time.
Reframing a task as a choice ('I get to') instead of an obligation ('I have to') reduces resistance.
Long-term goals stay motivating when broken into milestones you can actually see progress toward.
Consistency beats intensity - showing up daily for 10 minutes outperforms a rare 3-hour burst.
Your environment shapes behavior more than willpower does - reduce friction to the task you want to do.
